name: Shoshoni
short_description: BLM public rockhounding near Shoshoni with excellent opportunities for agate, jasper, and chalcedony. Free collecting on gentle terrain with no permits required.
description:
Shoshoni BLM lands in Fremont County offer accessible rockhounding for collectors of all skill levels seeking quality agates, jaspers, and chalcedony specimens. Located just 3 miles from the town of Shoshoni at 5,218 feet elevation, this site features gentle slopes and open terrain ideal for surface collecting without the need for specialized equipment or permits.

The geology of this region reflects Wyoming's Basin and Range province influences combined with Laramide-age formations. Miocene volcanic and hydrothermal activity deposited silica-rich materials throughout the Absaroka Range foothills, creating the microcrystalline quartz deposits found here today. The Shoshoni area sits within a zone where weathering and erosion have exposed these colorful specimens across benches and washes.

Collectors typically find banded agates, red and orange jasper nodules, and translucent chalcedony pieces weathering from hillsides and eroding from stream cuts. The gentle topography makes this an excellent location for families and beginners who want quality material without demanding physical exertion. Most productive areas are within short walking distance of parking pullouts along access roads.

As BLM land, this location operates under standard recreational collection guidelines - personal use only, no commercial activity, and a 25-pound daily limit per Federal Land Policy and Management Act (FLPMA) regulations. The open desert environment means excellent visibility for spotting specimens, though the exposed terrain offers minimal shade.
history: The Shoshoni area sits within the Absaroka Range foothills where Eocene through Miocene volcanic and hydrothermal systems deposited extensive silica-rich mineralizing fluids. This geological activity created the agate and jasper deposits that weather out across the gentle slopes and badlands terrain visible today, making the region a natural collecting destination for rockhounds seeking high-quality microcrystalline quartz specimens.
tips: Search the weathered hillsides and drainage benches where erosion exposes fresh specimens - agates often show distinctive banding patterns and translucent edges. Bring a 5-gallon bucket and collection bag to stay organized, as the gentle terrain encourages thorough exploration of larger areas. Look for color variations from pale white to deep red in jasper nodules; the best specimens often weather loose on slopes rather than requiring extraction. Early morning or late afternoon light helps spot the subtle translucence that distinguishes quality chalcedony from common quartz.
terrain_notes: Elevation of 5,218 feet on gentle rolling slopes with sparse sagebrush and short grass cover. Terrain is relatively open with good visibility and minimal obstacles. Most collecting areas require 10 to 30 minutes of easy walking from parking areas. Footing is generally firm desert soil with scattered loose rock, making hiking boots optional but recommended for comfort.
best_season: April - October. Late spring through early fall provides optimal conditions with mild temperatures and dry trails. Summer heat (June-August) can be intense at this elevation; bring extra water. Avoid November through March when snow and cold temperatures are common at 5,200+ feet elevation.
written_directions: From Shoshoni town center, head north on Wyoming State Highway 120 for approximately 2 miles. Turn east onto County Road 4 (Tensleep Creek Road) and continue for 1 mile to multiple informal pullouts on BLM land. GPS coordinates 43.202309, -108.064123 mark the primary collecting area. The road surface is well-maintained gravel suitable for passenger vehicles under normal conditions.
road_conditions: Maintained gravel road, suitable for passenger vehicles. No high-clearance required.
parking_notes: Multiple informal pullouts along County Road 4 with space for 3-5 vehicles per location. No developed parking lot; pull completely off the road.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation. Pack out all trash. Respect private property boundaries.
rules:
Personal use only
Maximum 25 pounds per day per FLPMA

No motorized excavation or heavy equipment
Pack out all trash
Respect posted private property boundaries
nearest_services: Shoshoni town (3 miles) offers gas, food, and water. General store and basic supplies available.
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat terrain with gentle slopes accessible to most hikers and families. Gravel access road suitable for standard vehicles. No wheelchair accessibility on collecting areas due to uneven natural terrain. Sturdy shoes recommended but not essential for casual surface collecting.

faq.question: What gemstones can I find rockhounding near Shoshoni Wyoming?
faq.answer: The Shoshoni BLM area is known for quality agate, jasper, chalcedony, and quartz specimens. Agates typically display banding patterns in white, red, and brown; jasper occurs in red, orange, and mottled varieties; and chalcedony appears as translucent waxy specimens. Most material weathers loose on slopes rather than requiring extraction.

faq.question: Is rockhounding at Shoshoni free or do I need a permit?
faq.answer: Rockhounding at Shoshoni is completely free with no permit required. As BLM public land, recreational collecting under FLPMA allows personal use collection of up to 25 pounds per day at no cost. Simply follow the collection rules - no commercial use, no motorized equipment, and pack out your trash.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to rockhound near Shoshoni?
faq.answer: April through October offers the best rockhounding conditions with mild temperatures and dry trails. Late spring and early fall are ideal - avoid the intense summer heat (June-August) without extra water, and steer clear of November through March when snow and cold weather are common at this 5,200-foot elevation.

faq.question: Is Shoshoni rockhounding site beginner friendly and good for families?
faq.answer: Yes, absolutely. The Shoshoni location features gentle rolling slopes, easy terrain, and excellent surface collecting with no specialized equipment needed. Children ages 6 and up can participate comfortably. The site is perfect for families looking to learn rockhounding basics while finding quality specimens.

faq.question: How do I find the best agate and jasper at Shoshoni rockhounding area?
faq.answer: Focus your search on weathered hillsides and drainage benches where erosion naturally exposes specimens. Look for color variations and banding patterns on loose material rather than extracting from solid rock. Early morning or late afternoon light helps identify the subtle translucence of quality chalcedony. The gentle terrain allows you to thoroughly explore larger areas.

faq.question: Can I bring my dog rockhounding at Shoshoni Wyoming?
faq.answer: Yes, dogs are welcome on this BLM public land. The open terrain and gentle slopes are dog-friendly, and the short 3-mile distance from town makes it convenient for a half-day outing with your pet. Bring extra water for both you and your dog since the site offers no shade.
